speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, an interview with Dr. Michael Okunieff, March 27, 1996. So you--you were saying that the Endecki came in with scissors into a railway compartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, on the train. And they started to cut the beards of the Hasidim. My father was a strong man. Stand up, and he started to put up a fight against-- against a few of the hoodlums. And one who should understand said this was not an even fight. So they took the scissor and they cut him up the face. And after this incident, they brought him into to-- an ambulatory, or to first aid, and he was bandaged up. And he came in-- in the-- he came back to Vilna and told the story. And this incident, I remember, when he mentioned to my mother, maybe we could leave to the-- the-- to a greener pasture, to leave Vilna. But the bridges were closed. Even my mother had education-- she finished gymnasium in Russia with a gold medal. And she was considered over there, almost very-- she was considered an educated woman. And she was continuously reading and at that time that really something-- something a little bit more progressive. Now, I would like to mention another incident. And this incident is-- probably remains in my mind. And this was in the summer of 1935. We used to go out for the summer to a dacha-- a dacha is a summer home. And over there, we used to spend the-- the time. My father used to stay in town and produce and help to build and produce the goods-- ware, the glass, and whatever. And we children used to come in once in a while to help out. And we always helped out. We used to take the unfinished product to let it develop. And once upon a time in the summer, I was curious. We were in [NON-ENGLISH]. And over there, over the bridge was-- was [NON-ENGLISH]. And at the head of what's-- [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] we call this, like a church.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was how far away from Vilna, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five miles, four and one half miles. You had to go along the river, Vilna. And I was curious, always what they have over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to where that road and after that through-- through some bushes and almost a wood. It was almost half a mile, we saw a church. In front of the church was a statue laying from-- from Jesus, with open-- with open cuts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the worshipers, after-- after the ceremony, or whatever, used to go out and around kneel around the-- the statute. The statue was not hanging, but was laying. I hide myself in between the bushes and I was looking. And the priest who was there denounced the Jews as was heretics, as the killers of Jesus. And-- and I was ready-- when I was ready to run away, they saw me. They started to go after me. And at the time, they did not like it, that somebody else is watching them in their church. And I never really attended church till the Russian came in. I took to going inside, even the cathedral. And they put a-- put a chase after me, and I barely escaped with-- with my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I wanted to say is in here we have two forces over there. One is the force from the-- from the church, what is advocating-- what was advocating hate. What means hate? Superiority of their religion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alternately, from the other side and on the other side we have the Jewish religion. And what taught us to respect human dignity and-- and following God's-- in God's path. And this probably has a lot to do with our problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as the beginning of the war in '39, do you remember the attack on Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would like to make a parallel. Once upon a time, there was-- there was a Hasid, and he was on the-- in the desert. And while walking, he saw two pair of shadows. One pair his own, and one pair of God. And this pair-- and he show the shadows following him. In the beginning when everything was good, praise and bar mitzvah, and the good time. And soon, when we reach the war, one shadow disappears, from God.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they started all the headache and trouble, and when he went through, when he turned around and when he lost-- and happened to him, he lost his family, he lost his house, he lost his freedom. He was about to get killed. And he did not have-- and he turned around and asked God, where were you? How come you're not following me?

speaker: interviewee
text: God said, I was in front of you. I try to lift you up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while going through this second stage of my life, where I needed some lifting. And namely now, now the German are attacking Vilna. They're attacking-- when they're attacking-- you saw in the beginning, Stukas are--

speaker: interviewee
text: oh, when they-- in '39, there was not an attack. There was only a few airplanes coming overhead, Stukas. And they mobilized my brother to defend it. But before we had a chance, it was all over and the Russian, on the third or fourth day, came in Vilna. And the culture, now the-- this was a different life. This was a transformation, from a youth, from an innocent, I'm starting to get to see-- already, I finish already college, gymnasium. And this was a place where they let us develop. We had a teacher at that time from Yiddish in gymnasium. He was the head of Bund. And he introduced the feeling-- kinder, he said, I see it-- I see the war, but keep your respect. Be strong even when you're-- when you're eating, cleaning. And before you notice-- before you notice, you have changes from the Russian are moving-- moving out. The Lithuanians are coming. The Lithuanians, but the Russians didn't trust the Lithuanians to come back to Vilna, in-- in a-- and stuck in here only a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- the Russian occupation lasted then, for about a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Less.

speaker: interviewer
text: Less than a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight months, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the Russian occupation of Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way, materially, it really was very good. We had some-- and my father was producing the gloves and the leather and got rich. Emotionally, this was a time what you felt a lot of development. Why, the first time what you could-- could go out on the street and be human. And the idea of it was to give us a substitute almost for our deep religious conviction. But dialectic materialism was a new idea. Was in here the-- the the Marxism, the equality, the-- the-- even the dictatorship of the proletariat, which nobody understood what does it mean. But something new to digest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was close to us because it was in here now, the workers should get the same deal. But I want to mention something in here. The Russian did-- agitators-- what mean agitators? They used the youth as a tool. And they send us out to make a census in the city. But then they could go out to the city, they send us to the lower, to the Christian side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened was we saw how the Christians were living. We saw the prostitution. We were still kids, prostitution, the drunkards, we saw all that was there, the injustice, what a society, a poor society produced. And after that, I realized that Hitler could release the anger, the energy to destroy the Jewry from the same people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was a businessman. Was any action taken against him by the Russians because he was a businessman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. For instance-- for instance, we had to build a melina. Melina is a hiding place. In the melina, we slept many nights in the attic. Why? Because he was, it made-- made-- he was according to the Russian, bourgeoisie, a rich man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wanted us, since they came in, just before they moved out, they wanted-- they took-- they wanted to take us and to send us to Russia. And since that time, he was hiding himself a few weeks. And after that, the Russians moved out from the house. So luckily-- I wish they would grab us and he would be still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But life has its own story. So this is one of the stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then came the German attack in June of 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I would like to bring in here a little philosophy. And namely, Malachi said, do not fear--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the prophet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Malachi, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Malachi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Malachi, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do not fear the wrath of God. Do fear the wrath of men. The German are coming in with an idea where they confer moral value on physical force. They came in with such a power, with panic, with destruction. And-- and for days, the-- the-- the army was traveling and they was-- before you notice, they started to kill, to produce some terror, panic, fear. This was three, maybe they dread what, from the very beginning they introduced in the-- against the-- the civilian population. And now I would like to bring a little-- my own story. The Germans mobilized all these elements that I was talking about and especially the Lithuanians and they organized a [NON-ENGLISH]-- what was a militia. And anyway, there was a lot of friction people in the Poles and the Lithuanian. The same Poles, what during the-- the Polish regiment, when the Polacks were over there, were Polish. But the moment-- the moment the German came in, it was like turn coat. They became Lithuanian. And they took over the government. And they joined forces with the German. And no wonder the first victim were the Jews. So they take the Jews in the beginning and the forces grab the Jews and force them in labor groups to clean up the city. And after that, by the same time, instead of sending them back home, they were taking them to work in Ponary, where they used to destroy them. And I would like to tell a story what happened to me. In the first six weeks of the war, the German took in about 5 million POW.

speaker: interviewer
text: Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian POW. And the thing is-- and they went on their-- almost on their own will, because Stalin-- they objected against Stalin. But what happened? Instead of treating them human, they put them in cattle-- in cattle cars to work in the behind-- without food, without help, live without water, on starvation. And let them die by starvation. And this is what I want to tell the story. While the German came in, once upon a time the German demanded some workers for the banhoff or the train. The train-- to change in the beginning, we had to change from the-- from the large tracks to the small tracks-- to the European tracks. And we used to work eight hours a day. And well, I was-- I joined-- they called me in, I went over there, and I did all the work. And usually the train used to stop on the banhoff of Vilna, what's a cross section. And they used to open the doors and throw out the dead bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of these Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the Russian POWs. And we use to make some holes and just dig a hole and just bury them. Once upon a time, they opened the door, they threw out a couple of dead bodies, and suddenly, a POW runs out, he runs! And the German gendarme starts to scream, stop, halt, halt, or stop, stop. And we were looking in here. He didn't stop. Over there was a woman, a Polish woman, maybe some Belarus, used to come over and with a loaf of bread and threw this to the POW. He didn't stop. He was running to her in spite of the warning. And he was so hungry, the-- the feeling of the hunger and-- and the bread, and to get food was so strong that he was-- that he was sacrificed his life. He ran to the woman and before-- even before he left, she grabs the bread. The German took out the pistol and just killed her-- killed him. And we had to dig a hole to-- and to bury him. And I remember myself, after I bury him, I put a stone, just for a reminder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And later-- later , on you felt the hunger that he had felt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. Now, the building-- the second stage, is-- what-- what the German are doing or making is the [NON-ENGLISH], this mean provocation. They bring a dead body of a German and claim that the Jews did it. And they clean up the area where they later are arranged the ghetto. And now we have a ghetto. This was almost from the time-- from the time, of conquering Vilna to the ghetto is about six weeks. During the six weeks, we have different codes, putting on the yellow-- the yellow Star of David, working in the street, to give up, our whatever, the money, to give up the-- the-- the-- the different-- whatever possessions we have. And now we have the ghetto. And this is another story what I want to tell. They give us only one hour to prepare. We were living on-- on [NON-ENGLISH] 5. And they give us only one hour to prepare to go to the ghetto. This is the area where the took the 15,000 Jews before and took out, under the provocation and took them to Ponary and killed them. And we went with our clothes over there through Marcova, and to Litska. And from Litska, this was when we went into the ghetto. The ghetto was only five streets. The ghetto was, I would say, about, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] almost like a very small area. From one side, we have the main streets of [NON-ENGLISH], what was a border, Litska from the other side, and [NON-ENGLISH] on the-- on the main-- main street. We went across to Litska. From the back onto Litska, was a Lithuanian garrison. They let us in through-- there was openings in Litska between Litska and [NON-ENGLISH]. And over there, they let a certain amount of people in into the openings. Luckily we were one of the first. And the rest of them, instead of-- they just look directly to Lukiskes prison, and from Lukiskes prison, they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, for days they keep them in the prison. And every day, they used to take a couple thousand people, bring them into Ponary and finish them up. But as if that wasn't enough of that, the next day, next morning, they came in, into Litska and they cleaned up everybody from, Litska.

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily we were over there, so instead of remaining in Litska, we found over there, we went through the attic, and we went to [NON-ENGLISH]. And we remained in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And this [NON-ENGLISH], this was a different street?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next street. But it was a part of the street. Why? Because Litska is like the-- they hammered down, and this was the border and afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the thing is, now we came in, we didn't have almost anything. And my father decided to go back to the house and to save some of the stuff. Just before we left together, he built a hiding place.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we has a lot of goods and clothes, et cetera. He made a hole in the-- in the-- in the-- in one of the places. And he hide some of the clothes. In addition, he builded-- he builded over there-- in-- in the wall, he hided the gold, silver, and the different-- the different jewelery, what he wanted to-- to hide. And he was thinking to bring it, to get it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at night, he went under the darkness of night, he hide it, and came in, in the-- to the-- to the building outside of the ghetto. We're going to stop here and then go to another tape.
